title: Giant Cell Arteritis
oneLiner: Vasculitis of medium/large arteries in patients >50, causing headache, jaw claudication, visual loss – treat with high-dose steroids immediately to prevent blindness.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Irreversible bilateral blindness:** Ask about sudden vision loss or transient visual obscurations (amaurosis fugax). Fundoscopy may show pale, swollen optic disc (anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy) or normal. Immediate high-dose steroids can salvage the second eye.
- **Posterior circulation stroke / aortic dissection/tamponade:** GCA can involve vertebral arteries leading to brainstem stroke, or cause aortic aneurysm rupture. Look for new neurological signs (diplopia, vertigo, ataxia) or chest/back pain with hypotension – needs urgent CT angiogram.
sections.Presentation:
- Unilateral or bilateral new-onset headache (often temporal, but can be frontal/occipital)
- Scalp tenderness: pain when brushing hair or wearing glasses
- Jaw claudication: pain on chewing (highly specific)
- Visual symptoms: amaurosis fugax (fleeting monocular loss), diplopia, partial or complete vision loss
- Systemic: malaise, weight loss, night sweats, fever, polymyalgia rheumatica (stiffness/pain in shoulders, hips)
- Exam: tender, nodular, pulseless temporal artery (may be normal); fundoscopy: optic disc oedema/pallor, retinal haemorrhages; proximal muscle tenderness (if PMR overlap)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Tension / migraine headache:** No jaw claudication, no systemic symptoms, temporal arteries normal.
- **Trigeminal neuralgia:** Electric shock-like pain, no constitutional upset.
- **Temporomandibular joint dysfunction:** Clicking, limited mouth opening, no visual symptoms.
- **Subarachnoid haemorrhage:** Thunderclap headache, neck stiffness, vomiting – CT head immediately, LP if negative.
- **Herpes zoster (shingles):** Pain precedes rash, vesicles appear after a few days.
- **Cervical spondylosis / neck pain:** Pain radiating from neck, no jaw claudication or temporal tenderness.
- **Infectious endocarditis:** Fever, heart murmur, embolic phenomena – blood cultures, echo.
- **Malignancy:** Weight loss, night sweats – CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, tumour markers.

**Splitter:** Jaw claudication – highly specific for GCA. If patient has headache + jaw pain on chewing, treat as GCA until proven otherwise.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Visual acuity each eye, relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD), fundoscopy (pale swollen disc or normal). Check temporal artery pulses.
- **Bloods:** FBC (normocytic anaemia, thrombocytosis), ESR (usually >50, often >100 mm/hr), CRP (elevated), LFTs (mildly raised transaminases, ALP).
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: Temporal artery ultrasound – look for halo sign (hypoechoic circumferential wall thickening) – highly specific if present.  
  - Second-line: High-resolution MRI brain with contrast (can show vessel wall enhancement) if US inconclusive.  
  - CTPA or echocardiogram if suspicion of aortic involvement (chest pain, widened mediastinum).
- **Special tests:** Temporal artery biopsy (gold standard) – do within 1 week, ideally before steroids but **never delay steroids** for biopsy. Sample 3–5 cm of artery, send fixed in formalin. Negative biopsy does not rule out GCA (skip lesions).
- **Diagnostic criteria:**  
  American College of Rheumatology 1990 – 3 of 5: age ≥50, new headache, temporal artery abnormality, ESR ≥50, abnormal biopsy. Sensible clinical approach: age >50 + ESR/CRP markedly elevated + typical symptoms = start steroids.
- **Never-miss test:** **Immediate ESR and CRP** – a normal ESR/CRP makes GCA very unlikely but does not exclude it. If strongly suspicious, treat anyway.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If any visual symptoms (amaurosis fugax, diplopia, vision loss) or high clinical suspicion: give **prednisolone 60 mg** immediately (or IV methylprednisolone 500–1000 mg if vision already affected). Do not wait for investigations.  
  - Urgent ophthalmology referral if visual symptoms.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Continue high-dose oral prednisolone 40–60 mg daily.  
  - Arrange temporal artery ultrasound or biopsy within 1 week.  
  - Start bone protection: alendronic acid 70 mg weekly (if eGFR >35) + calcium/vitamin D (Adcal D3 one tablet BD).  
  - Proton pump inhibitor: omeprazole 20 mg daily or lansoprazole 15 mg daily.  
  - Monitor visual acuity daily. If new visual loss, escalate to senior and consider IV methylprednisolone.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Slow steroid taper over 12–18 months: reduce by 2.5–5 mg every 2–4 weeks initially, slower below 15 mg.  
  - Monitor symptoms (headache, jaw claudication, vision) and inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP) – aim to treat to clinical response, not normalised ESR.  
  - Consider disease-modifying agents (methotrexate, tocilizumab) if unable to wean off steroids or frequent relapses – refer to rheumatology.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** No specific – admit for monitoring/treatment if vision threatened, severe symptoms, or need for IV steroids. Otherwise, consider outpatient management with daily review until response.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line:** Prednisolone 40–60 mg PO once daily (starting dose). Reduce after 2–4 weeks to 30 mg, then taper.  
- **If visual loss at presentation:** IV methylprednisolone 500 mg–1 g daily for 3 days, then switch to oral prednisolone 40–60 mg.  
- **Bone protection:** Alendronic acid 70 mg PO weekly (take with full glass of water, sit upright 30 min) + calcium 500 mg / vitamin D 400 IU (Adcal D3) one tablet BD.  
- **GI prophylaxis:** Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily or lansoprazole 15 mg PO daily.  
- **Common alternatives:** Methotrexate (10–20 mg weekly, with folic acid 5 mg weekly), tocilizumab (subcutaneous 162 mg weekly or IV 8 mg/kg every 4 weeks) – only under rheumatology supervision.  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** NSAIDs should be avoided (no proven efficacy for GCA, risk of renal and GI injury). Steroids: caution with diabetes (monitor glucose), hypertension, osteoporosis, infections.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose adjustment needed for steroids. Alendronic acid contraindicated if eGFR <35 mL/min.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Delaying steroids** for biopsy or imaging – 20% of untreated GCA patients go blind in one eye within 2 weeks, and many of the rest within months. **Do not wait.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Irreversible vision loss (anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy), central retinal artery occlusion, posterior circulation stroke, aortic dissection/aneurysm, myocardial infarction (very rare).  
- **Chronic:** Steroid-induced: osteoporosis, avascular necrosis, diabetes, Cushing’s syndrome, cataracts, infection risk, proximal myopathy. Aortic aneurysm (develops years later – annual surveillance with echo/CT).
sections.Prognosis: - With prompt high-dose steroids: rapid improvement (days), <5% new vision loss if treated within 24–48 hours. Relapse rate 40–50% during taper. Mortality same as general population if treated, but increased if aortic involvement develops. Most patients eventually taper off steroids. Delay >1 week in treatment increases risk of permanent visual loss.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Start steroids immediately if clinical suspicion is high** (especially jaw claudication + elevated ESR/CRP). Write and give the first dose in ED.  
- **Always check inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP) –** if both normal, GCA is unlikely but not impossible; discuss with rheumatology.  
- **Arrange temporal artery biopsy within 1 week** and label the specimen clearly – it remains diagnostic even after 2 weeks of steroids (lower yield).  
- **Check for symptoms of polymyalgia rheumatica** – stiffness/aching in shoulders, hips – if present, steroid dose sufficient for both conditions.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give NSAIDs** for headache – no evidence, and they increase GI bleed risk when combined with steroids.  
- **Do not delay steroids for biopsy** – biopsy is gold standard but treat first, biopsy within 7 days.  
- **Do not overlook aortic involvement** – ask about chest/back pain, check for murmur, consider CXR or echo in all newly diagnosed GCA (aortic aneurysm may present years later).  
- **Do not stop steroids abruptly** – taper slowly to avoid adrenal insufficiency and relapse.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Any new visual symptoms (blurring, shadow, loss), chest or back pain (possible aortic dissection), severe headache, fever, or neurological symptoms (slurred speech, limb weakness, vertigo).  
- **Follow-up:** Rheumatology clinic within 2–4 weeks. GP for BP monitoring, bone density scan (DEXA) after 3–6 months. Ophthalmology review if visual symptoms.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior immediately if:** New or worsening visual loss despite steroids, severe headache not improving after 48 hours, suspicion of aortic dissection (chest/back pain, hypotension, widened mediastinum), or any acute neurological deficit.  
- **NEWS2 ≥5** or lactate >2 mmol/L suggests systemic complication (sepsis from steroid, or aortic catastrophe).  
- **ICU criteria:** Airway compromise from massive steroid-induced hyperglycaemia (rare), or aortic rupture.  
- **Crash call:** Unresponsive, profound hypotension (suspected rupture), or sudden vision loss in both eyes.

rawMarkdown:
## Giant Cell Arteritis

**One-liner:** Vasculitis of medium/large arteries in patients >50, causing headache, jaw claudication, visual loss – treat with high-dose steroids immediately to prevent blindness.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Irreversible bilateral blindness:** Ask about sudden vision loss or transient visual obscurations (amaurosis fugax). Fundoscopy may show pale, swollen optic disc (anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy) or normal. Immediate high-dose steroids can salvage the second eye.
- **Posterior circulation stroke / aortic dissection/tamponade:** GCA can involve vertebral arteries leading to brainstem stroke, or cause aortic aneurysm rupture. Look for new neurological signs (diplopia, vertigo, ataxia) or chest/back pain with hypotension – needs urgent CT angiogram.

### Presentation
- Unilateral or bilateral new-onset headache (often temporal, but can be frontal/occipital)
- Scalp tenderness: pain when brushing hair or wearing glasses
- Jaw claudication: pain on chewing (highly specific)
- Visual symptoms: amaurosis fugax (fleeting monocular loss), diplopia, partial or complete vision loss
- Systemic: malaise, weight loss, night sweats, fever, polymyalgia rheumatica (stiffness/pain in shoulders, hips)
- Exam: tender, nodular, pulseless temporal artery (may be normal); fundoscopy: optic disc oedema/pallor, retinal haemorrhages; proximal muscle tenderness (if PMR overlap)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Tension / migraine headache:** No jaw claudication, no systemic symptoms, temporal arteries normal.
- **Trigeminal neuralgia:** Electric shock-like pain, no constitutional upset.
- **Temporomandibular joint dysfunction:** Clicking, limited mouth opening, no visual symptoms.
- **Subarachnoid haemorrhage:** Thunderclap headache, neck stiffness, vomiting – CT head immediately, LP if negative.
- **Herpes zoster (shingles):** Pain precedes rash, vesicles appear after a few days.
- **Cervical spondylosis / neck pain:** Pain radiating from neck, no jaw claudication or temporal tenderness.
- **Infectious endocarditis:** Fever, heart murmur, embolic phenomena – blood cultures, echo.
- **Malignancy:** Weight loss, night sweats – CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, tumour markers.

**Splitter:** Jaw claudication – highly specific for GCA. If patient has headache + jaw pain on chewing, treat as GCA until proven otherwise.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Visual acuity each eye, relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD), fundoscopy (pale swollen disc or normal). Check temporal artery pulses.
- **Bloods:** FBC (normocytic anaemia, thrombocytosis), ESR (usually >50, often >100 mm/hr), CRP (elevated), LFTs (mildly raised transaminases, ALP).
- **Imaging:**  
  - First-line: Temporal artery ultrasound – look for halo sign (hypoechoic circumferential wall thickening) – highly specific if present.  
  - Second-line: High-resolution MRI brain with contrast (can show vessel wall enhancement) if US inconclusive.  
  - CTPA or echocardiogram if suspicion of aortic involvement (chest pain, widened mediastinum).
- **Special tests:** Temporal artery biopsy (gold standard) – do within 1 week, ideally before steroids but **never delay steroids** for biopsy. Sample 3–5 cm of artery, send fixed in formalin. Negative biopsy does not rule out GCA (skip lesions).
- **Diagnostic criteria:**  
  American College of Rheumatology 1990 – 3 of 5: age ≥50, new headache, temporal artery abnormality, ESR ≥50, abnormal biopsy. Sensible clinical approach: age >50 + ESR/CRP markedly elevated + typical symptoms = start steroids.
- **Never-miss test:** **Immediate ESR and CRP** – a normal ESR/CRP makes GCA very unlikely but does not exclude it. If strongly suspicious, treat anyway.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - If any visual symptoms (amaurosis fugax, diplopia, vision loss) or high clinical suspicion: give **prednisolone 60 mg** immediately (or IV methylprednisolone 500–1000 mg if vision already affected). Do not wait for investigations.  
  - Urgent ophthalmology referral if visual symptoms.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Continue high-dose oral prednisolone 40–60 mg daily.  
  - Arrange temporal artery ultrasound or biopsy within 1 week.  
  - Start bone protection: alendronic acid 70 mg weekly (if eGFR >35) + calcium/vitamin D (Adcal D3 one tablet BD).  
  - Proton pump inhibitor: omeprazole 20 mg daily or lansoprazole 15 mg daily.  
  - Monitor visual acuity daily. If new visual loss, escalate to senior and consider IV methylprednisolone.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Slow steroid taper over 12–18 months: reduce by 2.5–5 mg every 2–4 weeks initially, slower below 15 mg.  
  - Monitor symptoms (headache, jaw claudication, vision) and inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP) – aim to treat to clinical response, not normalised ESR.  
  - Consider disease-modifying agents (methotrexate, tocilizumab) if unable to wean off steroids or frequent relapses – refer to rheumatology.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** No specific – admit for monitoring/treatment if vision threatened, severe symptoms, or need for IV steroids. Otherwise, consider outpatient management with daily review until response.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line:** Prednisolone 40–60 mg PO once daily (starting dose). Reduce after 2–4 weeks to 30 mg, then taper.  
- **If visual loss at presentation:** IV methylprednisolone 500 mg–1 g daily for 3 days, then switch to oral prednisolone 40–60 mg.  
- **Bone protection:** Alendronic acid 70 mg PO weekly (take with full glass of water, sit upright 30 min) + calcium 500 mg / vitamin D 400 IU (Adcal D3) one tablet BD.  
- **GI prophylaxis:** Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily or lansoprazole 15 mg PO daily.  
- **Common alternatives:** Methotrexate (10–20 mg weekly, with folic acid 5 mg weekly), tocilizumab (subcutaneous 162 mg weekly or IV 8 mg/kg every 4 weeks) – only under rheumatology supervision.  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:** NSAIDs should be avoided (no proven efficacy for GCA, risk of renal and GI injury). Steroids: caution with diabetes (monitor glucose), hypertension, osteoporosis, infections.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose adjustment needed for steroids. Alendronic acid contraindicated if eGFR <35 mL/min.  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Delaying steroids** for biopsy or imaging – 20% of untreated GCA patients go blind in one eye within 2 weeks, and many of the rest within months. **Do not wait.**

### Complications
- **Acute:** Irreversible vision loss (anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy), central retinal artery occlusion, posterior circulation stroke, aortic dissection/aneurysm, myocardial infarction (very rare).  
- **Chronic:** Steroid-induced: osteoporosis, avascular necrosis, diabetes, Cushing’s syndrome, cataracts, infection risk, proximal myopathy. Aortic aneurysm (develops years later – annual surveillance with echo/CT).

### Prognosis
- With prompt high-dose steroids: rapid improvement (days), <5% new vision loss if treated within 24–48 hours. Relapse rate 40–50% during taper. Mortality same as general population if treated, but increased if aortic involvement develops. Most patients eventually taper off steroids. Delay >1 week in treatment increases risk of permanent visual loss.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Start steroids immediately if clinical suspicion is high** (especially jaw claudication + elevated ESR/CRP). Write and give the first dose in ED.  
- **Always check inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP) –** if both normal, GCA is unlikely but not impossible; discuss with rheumatology.  
- **Arrange temporal artery biopsy within 1 week** and label the specimen clearly – it remains diagnostic even after 2 weeks of steroids (lower yield).  
- **Check for symptoms of polymyalgia rheumatica** – stiffness/aching in shoulders, hips – if present, steroid dose sufficient for both conditions.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give NSAIDs** for headache – no evidence, and they increase GI bleed risk when combined with steroids.  
- **Do not delay steroids for biopsy** – biopsy is gold standard but treat first, biopsy within 7 days.  
- **Do not overlook aortic involvement** – ask about chest/back pain, check for murmur, consider CXR or echo in all newly diagnosed GCA (aortic aneurysm may present years later).  
- **Do not stop steroids abruptly** – taper slowly to avoid adrenal insufficiency and relapse.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Any new visual symptoms (blurring, shadow, loss), chest or back pain (possible aortic dissection), severe headache, fever, or neurological symptoms (slurred speech, limb weakness, vertigo).  
- **Follow-up:** Rheumatology clinic within 2–4 weeks. GP for BP monitoring, bone density scan (DEXA) after 3–6 months. Ophthalmology review if visual symptoms.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior immediately if:** New or worsening visual loss despite steroids, severe headache not improving after 48 hours, suspicion of aortic dissection (chest/back pain, hypotension, widened mediastinum), or any acute neurological deficit.  
- **NEWS2 ≥5** or lactate >2 mmol/L suggests systemic complication (sepsis from steroid, or aortic catastrophe).  
- **ICU criteria:** Airway compromise from massive steroid-induced hyperglycaemia (rare), or aortic rupture.  
- **Crash call:** Unresponsive, profound hypotension (suspected rupture), or sudden vision loss in both eyes.
